sections.Physiology:
Blood pressure is the product of systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and cardiac output (CO), which is in turn the product of heart rate and stroke volume.

MAP = CO x SVR | CO = HR x SV

Maintaining blood flow is of greater importance for adequate organ perfusion than blood pressure. In the context of a profoundly under-filled circulation, administration of vasoconstrictive drugs may achieve a higher MAP through increasing SVR, yet blood flow remains significantly impaired unless the intravascular volume is replaced.

Clinically relevant hypotension occurs when blood pressure falls below the threshold required to maintain flow to vital organs — estimated as 75% of the patient's normal resting systolic BP. This threshold is higher in patients with chronic hypertension due to upward shift of the autoregulatory range caused by adaptation of narrowed, atherosclerotic vessels.

Blood pressure is regulated by a fast neural response and a slower hormonal response. Sensory information is detected by baroreceptors (stretch receptors) in the carotid sinus and aortic arch. The vasomotor centre (VMC) in the medulla regulates blood pressure. Increased baroreceptor firing INHIBITS the VMC, lowering blood pressure. Sympathetic stimulation via α1-adrenoceptors causes vasoconstriction. α1-adrenoceptors are present in high density in skin, gut, and kidneys — but NOT in the brain or heart — thereby diverting blood toward vital organs.

Key hormones: adrenaline/noradrenaline (adrenal medulla), vasopressin (hypothalamus), angiotensin II (RAAS). Histamine and nitric oxide are potent vasodilators. In diabetic autonomic neuropathy, impaired sympathetic baroreceptor responses can cause postural hypotension.
sections.Monitoring:
Blood pressure can be measured non-invasively (NIBP) or invasively (intra-arterial BP, IABP). Erroneous low NIBP readings can result from a cuff that is too large. Arrhythmias and shivering also impair non-invasive measurement. Invasive arterial monitoring provides beat-to-beat accuracy and is indicated in severe hypotension; risks include digital ischaemia, aneurysm, and infection.

Cardiac output measurement methods:

Invasive — Pulmonary artery catheter (PAC): gold standard; thermodilution or dye dilution. Routine use has declined due to insertion risks and lack of mortality benefit in RCTs. Arterial waveform analysis devices: PiCCO and LiDCO are calibrated; LiDCOrapid is uncalibrated. These derive CO from the morphology of the arterial pressure waveform.

Minimally invasive — Oesophageal Doppler: measures blood flow velocity in the descending thoracic aorta. Only approximately 70% of cardiac output passes through the descending aorta; the raw value must be corrected before reporting total CO.

Non-invasive — Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE): rapid bedside assessment of cardiac function, volume status, and fluid responsiveness; can exclude important causes of cardiogenic shock (e.g. cardiac tamponade).
sections.Shock:
Shock is defined as globally impaired tissue and organ perfusion inadequate for the metabolic needs of the body. It affects almost one third of ICU patients.

Clinical features: SBP generally <90 mmHg (usually with tachycardia); urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr; cold/clammy/cyanosis; altered mental state/confusion/reduced GCS; lactate >1.5 mmol/L. Rising respiratory rate is the EARLIEST sign of developing shock. Patients on beta-blockers may NOT mount a compensatory tachycardia.

Shock classification:

Distributive: Low SVR, flushed/vasodilated, normal or HIGH cardiac output. Causes: sepsis (most common, ~2/3 of all shock), anaphylaxis, neurogenic (spinal cord injury), cyanide toxicity, adrenal insufficiency.

Hypovolaemic: Cold and peripherally shut down, low CO. Causes: haemorrhage, GI loss (diarrhoea/vomiting), third space losses, burns.

Obstructive: May initially have NORMAL blood pressure; low CO. Causes: pulmonary embolus (PE), tension pneumothorax, pericardial tamponade.

Cardiogenic: Normal or high blood pressure; cold and peripherally shut down; low CO (CI <2.2 L/min/m²) with SBP <90 mmHg. Causes: ACS/myocardial infarction (most common), arrhythmias, cardiomyopathies (e.g. HOCM).

The last three types all typically present with low CO. Distributive shock differs — normal or high CO, with low SVR.
sections.Treatment:
All patients with shock require general ABCDE resuscitation to maximise oxygen delivery alongside treatment of the underlying cause.

Fluid resuscitation: NICE guidelines recommend 500 mL crystalloid boluses over 15 minutes with reassessment after each bolus. Passive leg raise (PLR) acts as a reversible autotransfusion (~300–500 mL), mobilising venous blood from the lower limbs to test preload responsiveness without administering IV fluid. Dynamic markers (PPV, SVV) >10% predict fluid responsiveness but are ONLY valid when: (1) sinus rhythm, (2) fully passive controlled mechanical ventilation, (3) tidal volume of 8 mL/kg. Static markers: CVP, ScvO₂.

Fluid choice: Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's, Plasma-Lyte) preferred — avoid the hyperchloraemic acidosis caused by large volumes of 0.9% saline. Trial evidence: CHEST 2012 — HES 130/0.42 vs saline: no 90-day mortality difference but HES increased renal failure and need for blood products. SAFE 2004 — albumin vs saline: no overall 28-day mortality difference; post-hoc trauma subgroup showed higher mortality with albumin. FEAST 2011 — bolus resuscitation with albumin or saline increased mortality in African children with severe infection. CRISTAL 2013 — colloids vs crystalloids: no 28-day mortality difference.

Vasopressors: Noradrenaline — first-line (SSC guidelines); predominant α1 activity with some β; central administration preferred to avoid extravasation tissue necrosis. Vasopressin — second-line; V1 receptor mechanism (NOT adrenergic); VASST and VANISH trials. Adrenaline — second-line vasopressor; more dysrhythmias than noradrenaline; α and β activity, dose-dependent. Dopamine — inferior to noradrenaline (more arrhythmias). Metaraminol/phenylephrine — less potent α1 agonists; short-term peri-operative use.

Inotropes: Dobutamine — β1 inotrope with β2 vasodilation; fall in MAP may require co-administration of vasopressor. Milrinone (PDE III inhibitor) — increases cAMP INDEPENDENTLY of adrenoceptors; useful when catecholamine receptor downregulation has occurred. Levosimendan — calcium sensitiser (sensitises troponin C to calcium); increases contractility WITHOUT increasing myocardial oxygen demand.

MAP targets: Standard ≥65 mmHg. Lower acceptable (permissive hypotension): haemorrhagic shock, perioperative hypotensive anaesthesia. Higher required: acute spinal cord injury (≥85–90 mmHg), raised intracranial pressure. Individualised approach essential.

GDT: Rivers et al. 2001 — single-centre RCT showing mortality benefit from EGDT targeting ScvO₂ ≥70%, CVP 8–12, MAP ≥65. ARISE, ProCESS, and ProMISe — three large multicentre RCTs — showed NO mortality benefit from routine EGDT vs usual care. Current evidence: individualised approach to haemodynamic targets; both excess and insufficient fluid cause morbidity.

questions.question: A 74-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension (baseline SBP approximately 185 mmHg) undergoes elective aortic valve replacement. In the post-operative ICU his MAP is 58 mmHg. The anaesthetist argues this is within acceptable limits for a post-operative patient. Which statement best explains why this MAP is most likely insufficient for this specific patient?
options: A: Aortic valve replacement increases myocardial oxygen demand, making the standard MAP target inadequate post-operatively.
B: MAP 58 mmHg falls below the universal minimum of 65 mmHg applied equally to all post-operative patients.
C: Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulatory range upward, requiring a higher MAP to perfuse narrowed vessels
D: Long-term antihypertensive therapy blunts baroreceptor sensitivity, predisposing to refractory reflex hypotension.
E: Post-operative pain chronically activates the vasomotor centre, causing paradoxical MAP reduction after surgery.
questions.explanation: Patients with chronic hypertension have structural adaptation (arteriolar hypertrophy and narrowing) that shifts cerebral, renal, and coronary autoregulatory curves upward. A MAP adequate for a normotensive patient is insufficient to drive flow through these narrowed vessels. The minimum acceptable MAP is estimated as approximately 75% of the patient's normal resting SBP — here approximately 140 mmHg, well above 58 mmHg. Treating the standard MAP ≥65 mmHg threshold as a universal rule applied equally to all post-operative patients is the near-miss trap here — that threshold applies to the general population and cannot be used as a universal rule for hypertensive patients. Increased myocardial oxygen demand from the surgery itself, blunted baroreceptor sensitivity from antihypertensive therapy, and post-operative pain do not explain why this specific MAP is insufficient in the way the shifted autoregulatory range does.
questions.topic: autoregulation chronic hypertension MAP threshold organ perfusion

questions.question: A 58-year-old woman in septic shock has received only 500 mL crystalloid before noradrenaline 0.15 mcg/kg/min is started. MAP rises from 52 to 68 mmHg. Thirty minutes later lactate has increased from 3.4 to 4.8 mmol/L and urine output is <0.2 mL/kg/hr. What is the most likely explanation for deteriorating tissue perfusion despite an apparently adequate MAP?
options: A: Noradrenaline has caused direct myocardial depression via β1-receptor downregulation.
B: Noradrenaline raised SVR/MAP without restoring cardiac output in an underfilled circulation, so tissue flow stays impaired
C: The noradrenaline dose is insufficient; further dose escalation will restore end-organ perfusion.
D: Noradrenaline-induced mesenteric vasoconstriction alone accounts for the lactate rise, with other organ perfusion preserved.
E: Rising lactate reflects catecholamine-stimulated hepatic gluconeogenesis rather than tissue hypoxia.
questions.explanation: MAP = CO × SVR. Noradrenaline increases SVR and thereby MAP, but in an underfilled patient with inadequate preload, cardiac output and actual tissue blood flow remain severely impaired. Rising lactate and oliguria confirm end-organ hypoperfusion despite a target MAP — the classic trap of treating MAP as a reliable surrogate for flow. Believing further dose escalation will restore end-organ perfusion is the near-miss here: escalating the dose increases SVR further without addressing the underlying hypovolaemia; adequate fluid resuscitation must precede or accompany vasopressor use. Direct myocardial depression via receptor downregulation, isolated mesenteric vasoconstriction with other organs preserved, and catecholamine-driven gluconeogenesis as the sole explanation for the lactate rise are all less complete or accurate explanations than the combination of inadequate preload and impaired flow described here.
questions.topic: MAP SVR cardiac output vasopressor underfilled flow vs pressure

questions.question: A 67-year-old man in distributive shock is receiving pressure support ventilation (PSV 12 cmH₂O, PEEP 8 cmH₂O) with preserved spontaneous respiratory effort. His arterial line shows a pulse pressure variation (PPV) of 18%. The nurse asks whether this reliably indicates fluid responsiveness. What is the most appropriate response?
options: A: Yes — PPV >10% reliably predicts fluid responsiveness regardless of ventilation mode.
B: No — PPV is unreliable in distributive shock because low SVR artificially inflates the reading.
C: No — PPV is only valid in sinus rhythm with fully passive ventilation at 8 mL/kg; spontaneous effort invalidates it
D: No — PPV requires a pulmonary artery catheter rather than a peripheral arterial line to be measured accurately.
E: Yes — PPV >10% on PSV confirms significant preload dependence and warrants an immediate 500 mL bolus.
questions.explanation: PPV and SVV are only valid dynamic markers of fluid responsiveness when three conditions are simultaneously met: sinus rhythm, fully passive controlled mechanical ventilation with no spontaneous effort, and a tidal volume of 8 mL/kg. Spontaneous respiratory effort during PSV generates unpredictable intrathoracic pressure swings that invalidate the PPV signal, regardless of PEEP level. Believing PPV >10% reliably predicts fluid responsiveness regardless of ventilation mode is the most common near-miss here — PPV is frequently misapplied in partially ventilated patients. The unreliability comes from the spontaneous breathing effort invalidating the underlying assumptions, not from low SVR artificially inflating the reading, and PPV is measured from a peripheral arterial line, not a pulmonary artery catheter. A passive leg raise, which does not depend on ventilation mode, would be the appropriate test in this scenario — not an immediate empirical fluid bolus based on this unreliable PPV reading.
questions.topic: PPV SVV fluid responsiveness validity sinus rhythm controlled MV spontaneous effort invalid

questions.question: A 32-year-old man sustains a complete cervical spinal cord injury at C5 in a diving accident. In the emergency department his SBP is 72 mmHg, HR is 48 bpm, and his peripheries are warm and dry. Temperature is 37.1°C and a FAST scan is negative for haemorrhage. What type of shock is most likely?
options: A: Cardiogenic shock from direct spinal cord injury causing myocardial depression.
B: Distributive (neurogenic) shock from loss of descending sympathetic tone below the injury level.
C: Obstructive shock from spinal cord oedema extrinsically compressing the vena cava.
D: Hypovolaemic shock from occult haemorrhage causing reduced venous return.
E: Distributive (anaphylactic) shock from a contrast agent used during imaging.
questions.explanation: Neurogenic shock is a form of distributive shock caused by disruption of descending sympathetic outflow below a high spinal cord injury (above T6). Loss of arteriolar tone reduces SVR producing vasodilation and warm peripheries, while unopposed vagal tone produces bradycardia — distinguishing it from hypovolaemic shock where reflex tachycardia and cold, shut-down peripheries are expected. Active haemorrhage causing hypovolaemic shock must always be excluded first as the most important near-miss, but the combination of bradycardia and warm peripheries, with a negative FAST, makes neurogenic shock the working diagnosis here rather than occult haemorrhage. Direct myocardial depression from the spinal injury, extrinsic vena caval compression from cord oedema, and an anaphylactic reaction to contrast are all less consistent with this specific combination of bradycardia, warm dry peripheries, and a negative FAST.
questions.topic: neurogenic shock distributive sympathetic loss bradycardia warm peripheries spinal cord

questions.question: A 61-year-old woman undergoes primary PCI for anterior STEMI. Four hours later she is cool, mottled, and oliguric with a GCS of 13. MAP is 70 mmHg on noradrenaline 0.1 mcg/kg/min. Cardiac output monitoring shows CI 1.8 L/min/m². Which statement about her haemodynamic status is correct?
options: A: She meets haemodynamic criteria for cardiogenic shock — CI <2.2 with signs of end-organ hypoperfusion
B: Cardiogenic shock cannot be confirmed without pulmonary artery catheter measurement of pulmonary artery wedge pressure.
C: Dobutamine should be avoided because its vasodilatory effect will worsen her hypotension.
D: Cardiogenic shock requires SBP <90 mmHg off all vasopressors before the diagnosis can be confirmed.
E: She does not meet criteria for cardiogenic shock because MAP is ≥65 mmHg.
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock is defined haemodynamically by CI <2.2 L/min/m² in combination with systolic BP <90 mmHg and/or evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion (oliguria, mottling, altered mentation). Vasopressors maintaining MAP ≥65 mmHg do not exclude the diagnosis if CI is reduced and perfusion is clearly inadequate, so a MAP of 70 mmHg does not rule this out. Requiring SBP <90 mmHg off all vasopressors before the diagnosis can be confirmed would delay treatment inappropriately — this patient already meets criteria via reduced CI and end-organ hypoperfusion signs. Avoiding dobutamine because its vasodilatory effect will worsen hypotension is also a near-miss trap: dobutamine is the appropriate first-line inotrope here, and its vasodilatory effect can actually reduce afterload and improve CI in this setting. A pulmonary artery catheter is not required to confirm the diagnosis when CI monitoring and clinical signs of hypoperfusion are already available.
questions.topic: cardiogenic shock criteria CI 2.2 STEMI vasopressor MAP end-organ perfusion

questions.question: A 52-year-old man is in septic shock from a perforated duodenal ulcer. A surgical resident proposes administering 1,000 mL of 6% hydroxyethyl starch (HES 130/0.42) for rapid volume expansion. Which statement provides the strongest justification for NOT using HES in this patient?
options: A: HES is contraindicated in post-operative surgical patients due to impairment of anastomotic wound healing.
B: The SAFE trial demonstrated higher 28-day mortality with HES compared to 4% albumin.
C: HES is hyperosmolar and causes intracellular dehydration in patients who are already fluid-depleted.
D: HES causes hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis more frequently than balanced crystalloids at equivalent volumes.
E: The CHEST trial showed HES 130/0.42 significantly increased renal failure and RRT need versus saline
questions.explanation: The CHEST trial (2012; approximately 7,000 ICU patients) found no difference in 90-day mortality between HES 130/0.42 and 0.9% saline, but HES significantly increased the rates of acute kidney injury and need for renal replacement therapy and blood products. HES is therefore not recommended in critically ill patients. The SAFE trial actually compared albumin with 0.9% saline, not HES, so citing a SAFE-trial mortality comparison between HES and albumin misattributes that trial's design. HES is not specifically contraindicated due to impaired anastomotic wound healing as the primary evidence-based justification here, and hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis is more characteristic of large-volume 0.9% saline than of HES specifically — balanced crystalloids are preferred partly to avoid that saline-related complication, but it is not the strongest justification against HES itself. The osmotic/intracellular-dehydration framing is not the well-established mechanism of HES-related renal harm either — the CHEST trial's renal replacement therapy finding is the strongest, most direct justification for avoiding HES here.
questions.topic: HES CHEST trial renal failure RRT colloid contraindication sepsis

questions.question: Which of the following conditions does NOT cause obstructive shock?
options: A: Cardiac tamponade.
B: Severe dynamic hyperinflation (auto-PEEP) during mechanical ventilation.
C: Massive pulmonary embolism.
D: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM).
E: Tension pneumothorax.
questions.explanation: Obstructive shock results from a mechanical obstacle to cardiac filling or outflow external to the myocardium: classic causes are massive PE, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, and severe auto-PEEP, all of which impair venous return or right heart filling. HOCM causes cardiogenic shock through dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction due to intrinsic myocardial hypertrophy — an intracardiac pathology correctly classified as cardiogenic, not obstructive. The near-miss is tamponade vs HOCM: tamponade is extra-pericardial compression of the heart (obstructive), whereas HOCM is intrinsic myocardial pathology (cardiogenic).
questions.topic: obstructive shock NOT HOCM cardiogenic PE tamponade tension pneumothorax auto-PEEP

questions.question: An oesophageal Doppler probe is positioned in a mechanically ventilated patient and measures blood flow velocity in the descending thoracic aorta. Why must the raw flow value be corrected before it is reported as cardiac output?
options: A: Aortic cross-sectional area varies widely with body habitus and must be indexed to body surface area before any flow calculation.
B: The probe measures right ventricular output, which is 10–15% lower than left ventricular output.
C: Oesophageal Doppler measures peak systolic velocity only, which must be integrated over time using a duty-cycle correction factor.
D: Only ~70% of cardiac output passes through the descending thoracic aorta — the remainder supplies the head, neck, and upper limbs
E: Signal attenuation by oesophageal mucosa systematically underestimates velocity by approximately 15%.
questions.explanation: Oesophageal Doppler estimates cardiac output by measuring blood flow velocity in the descending thoracic aorta and multiplying by the estimated aortic cross-sectional area. However, only approximately 70% of total cardiac output passes through the descending thoracic aorta at the point the probe measures — the remainder is distributed to the brain, upper limbs, and coronary circulation via vessels that branch off proximal to the probe. The device therefore applies a correction, dividing measured descending aortic flow by 0.7, to estimate total cardiac output; this 70% distribution constant is the key systemic adjustment required. Indexing aortic cross-sectional area to body surface area, correcting for a right-ventricular-only measurement, and applying a duty-cycle correction for peak-systolic-velocity integration are genuine technical considerations in Doppler-derived cardiac output but are not this primary systemic correction, and signal attenuation by oesophageal mucosa is not a recognised, quantified source of systematic underestimation in the way the 70% distribution correction is.
questions.topic: oesophageal Doppler 70% descending aorta CO correction cardiac output

questions.question: A 66-year-old woman with vasodilatory septic shock requires noradrenaline. A peripheral 18G cannula is in situ while central venous access is being established. The nurse asks why central administration is strongly preferred. What is the most important patient safety reason?
options: A: Extravasation of noradrenaline from a peripheral cannula causes intense local vasoconstriction that can lead to tissue ischaemia and necrosis.
B: Regulatory guidelines prohibit peripheral administration of any vasoactive agent within the ICU.
C: Peripheral infusion causes a reflex bradycardia that cannot be safely managed without simultaneous central venous pressure monitoring.
D: Peripheral cannulas have insufficient flow rates to deliver noradrenaline at therapeutic concentrations.
E: First-pass metabolism in subcutaneous tissue significantly reduces the bioavailability of peripherally administered noradrenaline.
questions.explanation: Noradrenaline is a potent α1-adrenoceptor agonist. Extravasation from a peripheral cannula causes intense local vasoconstriction resulting in tissue ischaemia and necrosis — the primary safety concern driving the recommendation for central administration. Brief peripheral use while central access is established may be acceptable with close monitoring for extravasation, but this should be time-limited. Regulatory guidelines do not categorically prohibit all peripheral vasoactive administration in this way, peripheral cannulas can in fact deliver adequate concentrations, and reflex bradycardia is not a primary concern during vasodilatory shock requiring central venous pressure monitoring. Subcutaneous first-pass metabolism is also not a recognised, clinically significant limitation of peripheral noradrenaline administration — the extravasation-necrosis risk is the key safety issue.
questions.topic: noradrenaline central administration extravasation tissue necrosis α1 agonist safety

questions.question: A 73-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy and cardiogenic shock has been on dobutamine for 36 hours. Despite escalation to 15 mcg/kg/min, cardiac index has not improved and the team suspects β1-adrenoceptor downregulation. Which agent would be most appropriate to add?
options: A: Vasopressin — to restore vascular tone via V1 receptors and offset the vasodilatory component.
B: Milrinone — a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor that elevates intracellular cAMP independently of adrenoceptors.
C: Phenylephrine — a selective α1 agonist that will increase afterload and thereby improve cardiac output.
D: Adrenaline — to provide combined α and β receptor stimulation at high infusion rates.
E: Metaraminol — to counteract dobutamine-induced vasodilation by increasing SVR.
questions.explanation: Phosphodiesterase III inhibitors such as milrinone inhibit the breakdown of cyclic AMP, increasing intracellular cAMP and thereby enhancing contractility and vasodilation independently of adrenergic receptors. This makes milrinone the agent of choice when prolonged catecholamine exposure has caused β1-adrenoceptor downregulation rendering dobutamine ineffective. Adrenaline also acts via adrenoceptors and would face the same downregulation limitation as dobutamine, so it is not the preferred next step. Phenylephrine, a selective α1 agonist, would increase afterload via pure α1 agonism — in a failing ventricle this would reduce cardiac index further and is potentially harmful, not beneficial. Vasopressin and metaraminol both primarily add vasoconstriction rather than addressing the underlying receptor-downregulation problem limiting dobutamine's inotropic effect.
questions.topic: milrinone PDE3 inhibitor cAMP adrenoceptor independent downregulation dobutamine catecholamine resistant

questions.question: A 65-year-old woman with decompensated heart failure has a CI of 1.7 L/min/m² despite dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min. The intensivist proposes adding levosimendan. A medical student asks how levosimendan increases cardiac contractility without increasing myocardial oxygen demand. Which explanation is correct?
options: A: It directly inhibits the sodium-calcium exchanger, increasing intracellular calcium without requiring adrenoceptor activation.
B: It opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels, hyperpolarising the sarcolemma and thereby reducing resting energy consumption.
C: It inhibits phosphodiesterase III, preventing cAMP breakdown and augmenting calcium entry through L-type channels.
D: It sensitises troponin C to calcium, enhancing cross-bridge formation without increasing calcium cycling
E: It blocks β2-adrenoceptors, reducing catecholamine-mediated vasodilation and selectively redirecting coronary blood flow.
questions.explanation: Levosimendan is a calcium sensitiser: it binds to troponin C and stabilises the calcium-troponin C complex, enhancing the sensitivity of the contractile apparatus to existing calcium levels. Because it does not increase intracellular calcium load or cAMP, it avoids the energetic cost of increased calcium cycling — hence no rise in myocardial oxygen demand. Describing the mechanism as phosphodiesterase III inhibition preventing cAMP breakdown and augmenting calcium entry actually describes milrinone's mechanism, not levosimendan's, and is the most common mechanistic near-miss here. Direct inhibition of the sodium-calcium exchanger, opening of ATP-sensitive potassium channels, and β2-adrenoceptor blockade are not levosimendan's mechanism either. The distinction between calcium sensitisation (levosimendan) and cAMP-mediated effects (dobutamine/milrinone) is a key EDIC discriminator.
questions.topic: levosimendan calcium sensitiser troponin C no oxygen demand PDE3 near-miss

questions.question: In 2001, Rivers et al. published a single-centre RCT reporting significant mortality benefit from early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) targeting ScvO₂ ≥70%, CVP 8–12 mmHg, and MAP ≥65 mmHg in septic shock. Which combination of subsequent trials demonstrated no mortality benefit from routine protocolised EGDT compared to usual care?
options: A: SAFE, CHEST, and CRISTAL.
B: TRISS, NICE-SUGAR, and SUP-ICU.
C: VASST, VANISH, and CORTICUS.
D: FEAST, SPLIT, and PLUS.
E: ARISE, ProCESS, and ProMISe.
questions.explanation: ARISE (Australia/New Zealand, 2014), ProCESS (USA, 2014), and ProMISe (UK, 2015) were three large multicentre RCTs that each enrolled hundreds of patients with early septic shock and found no mortality benefit from protocolised EGDT compared to usual care, leading to abandonment of routine EGDT. SAFE, CHEST, and CRISTAL evaluated fluid type (albumin, HES, and colloids versus crystalloids respectively) rather than resuscitation protocols, as did FEAST, SPLIT, and PLUS (fluid bolus and balanced-versus-saline trials). TRISS, NICE-SUGAR, and SUP-ICU evaluated transfusion thresholds, glucose control, and stress ulcer prophylaxis respectively — unrelated to EGDT. VASST, VANISH, and CORTICUS are trials of vasopressors and corticosteroids in sepsis, not resuscitation protocols.
questions.topic: ARISE ProCESS ProMISe EGDT no mortality benefit Rivers individualised approach

questions.question: A 55-year-old man is admitted to ICU following trauma with a complete T6 spinal cord injury. He is haemodynamically unstable requiring vasopressor support. Which MAP target is most appropriate to preserve spinal cord perfusion and minimise secondary neurological injury?
options: A: MAP ≥75 mmHg — an intermediate target to balance cord perfusion against vasopressor side effects.
B: MAP ≥65 mmHg acutely, increasing to ≥85 mmHg only if neurological deterioration is documented.
C: MAP ≥85–90 mmHg — a higher target is recommended in acute spinal cord injury for cord perfusion
D: MAP ≥50 mmHg — permissive hypotension is acceptable in acute spinal cord injury.
E: MAP ≥65 mmHg — the standard ICU target should not be deviated from.
questions.explanation: In acute spinal cord injury, spinal cord perfusion pressure depends directly on MAP (analogous to cerebral perfusion pressure in TBI). Guidelines recommend maintaining MAP ≥85-90 mmHg for the first 5-7 days after injury to optimise perfusion and limit secondary ischaemic damage. This is one of the specific clinical scenarios where the standard MAP ≥65 mmHg target is deliberately exceeded, alongside raised intracranial pressure. Treating MAP 65 mmHg as the standard target that should not be deviated from is the near-miss trap here — appropriate for most critically ill patients but insufficient for neuroprotection in acute spinal cord injury. Escalating only reactively, once neurological deterioration is already documented, misses the point of the recommendation, which is prophylactic maintenance of the higher target from the outset; an intermediate target of 75 mmHg, or permissive hypotension down to 50 mmHg, both fall short of the recommended 85-90 mmHg range.
questions.topic: MAP target spinal cord injury 85-90 higher than standard neuroprotection ICP

questions.question: A 59-year-old man with suspected sepsis has MAP 61 mmHg. Before administering a fluid bolus, the registrar performs a passive leg raise (PLR) and observes a 14% increase in cardiac output. What is the key clinical advantage of PLR over a traditional 500 mL crystalloid bolus for assessing fluid responsiveness?
options: A: PLR can be performed and interpreted accurately without any haemodynamic monitoring device.
B: PLR is a reversible autotransfusion that tests preload responsiveness without giving IV fluid — fully reversible on lowering the legs
C: PLR avoids the risk of precipitous hypotension that routinely accompanies rapid crystalloid boluses in septic shock.
D: PLR provides a more precise quantification of cardiac output change than monitoring arterial pulse pressure during a fluid bolus.
E: PLR is faster to perform than a fluid challenge and therefore reduces time to initiation of vasopressors.
questions.explanation: The passive leg raise mobilises venous blood from the lower limb and splanchnic reservoir into the central circulation, providing a reversible preload challenge. If cardiac output rises, the patient is preload-responsive and a fluid bolus is likely to be beneficial. Critically, the effect is completely reversed when the legs are lowered — no net fluid is administered, unlike a traditional bolus. Believing PLR can be performed and interpreted accurately without any haemodynamic monitoring device is a near-miss: accurate CO measurement (arterial waveform analysis, Doppler) is required to detect the response; PLR cannot be reliably interpreted by clinical examination alone. PLR does not avoid a hypotension risk that doesn't routinely accompany careful crystalloid boluses in the first place, it is not inherently more precise than pulse-pressure monitoring during a bolus, and its main advantage is reversibility rather than simply being faster to perform.
questions.topic: passive leg raise reversible autotransfusion fluid responsiveness no net fluid

questions.question: Which of the following vasopressors does NOT exert its primary vasoconstrictive effect via α1-adrenoceptors?
options: A: Vasopressin.
B: Noradrenaline.
C: Phenylephrine.
D: Adrenaline at high infusion rates.
E: Metaraminol.
questions.explanation: Vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone) produces vasoconstriction by activating V1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle — a mechanism entirely independent of the adrenergic system. This is clinically significant because vasopressin retains efficacy in catecholamine-resistant shock where prolonged adrenoceptor stimulation has caused α1 downregulation. Noradrenaline, metaraminol, and phenylephrine all exert vasoconstriction predominantly via α1-adrenoceptors. Adrenaline at high doses also primarily activates α1-adrenoceptors (with β-activity predominating at lower doses). Vasopressin is therefore the only option operating through a fundamentally distinct receptor mechanism.
questions.topic: vasopressin V1 receptor NOT α1-adrenoceptor mechanism catecholamine independent

questions.question: Regarding the physiological determinants of mean arterial pressure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In an underfilled patient with septic shock, increasing SVR with noradrenaline will always restore adequate organ perfusion.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sympathetic stimulation via α1-adrenoceptors diverts blood away from the skin, gut, and kidneys toward the brain and heart.

statements.label: C
statements.text: α1-adrenoceptors are present in high density in the brain and heart, enabling preferential vasoconstriction of cerebral and coronary vessels during stress.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Mean arterial pressure equals cardiac output multiplied by systemic vascular resistance.

questions.explanation: Mean arterial pressure does equal cardiac output multiplied by systemic vascular resistance — the fundamental equation. Sympathetic stimulation via α1-adrenoceptors does divert blood away from the skin, gut, and kidneys toward the brain and heart, since α1-adrenoceptors are abundant in the skin, gut, and kidneys but are notably sparse in the brain and coronary circulation — this selective distribution is how sympathetic activation protects vital organs during haemodynamic stress. The claim that α1-adrenoceptors are present in high density in the brain and heart, enabling preferential vasoconstriction of cerebral and coronary vessels, is therefore false — it is the opposite distribution that protects these organs. The claim that increasing SVR with noradrenaline will always restore adequate organ perfusion in an underfilled septic patient is also false — in an underfilled circulation, noradrenaline increases SVR and MAP but cardiac output and tissue flow remain impaired without fluid resuscitation; pressure and flow are not equivalent.
questions.topic: MAP CO SVR α1 receptor distribution sympathetic diversion organ perfusion flow vs pressure

questions.question: Regarding the neural regulation of blood pressure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy can impair baroreceptor-mediated sympathetic responses, predisposing to postural hypotension and syncope.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Baroreceptors involved in blood pressure regulation are located in the carotid sinus and the aortic arch.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Increased baroreceptor firing stimulates the vasomotor centre (VMC) to raise blood pressure.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The neural baroreceptor-mediated response to acute hypotension is slower than the hormonal response mediated by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.

questions.explanation: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy can indeed impair baroreceptor-mediated sympathetic responses, predisposing to postural hypotension and syncope, since it disrupts the sympathetic efferent pathways that would normally blunt such drops in pressure. Baroreceptors involved in blood pressure regulation are located in the carotid sinus and the aortic arch, relaying afferent signals to the vasomotor centre in the medulla. The claim that increased baroreceptor firing stimulates the vasomotor centre to raise blood pressure is false and reversed — increased firing instead inhibits the VMC and reduces blood pressure; it is the regulatory brake on hypertension, not a stimulus to raise BP. The claim that the neural baroreceptor response is slower than the hormonal RAAS response is also false — the baroreceptor response is characteristically fast (milliseconds to seconds), whereas the RAAS hormonal response is comparatively slow (minutes to hours).
questions.topic: baroreceptors carotid aortic VMC inhibition neural fast hormonal slow diabetic autonomic neuropathy

questions.question: Regarding the clinical recognition and features of shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A serum lactate >1.5 mmol/L is consistent with inadequate tissue perfusion and supports a clinical diagnosis of shock.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Distributive shock typically presents with cold, clammy peripheries and a reduced cardiac output.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Patients receiving beta-blockers may fail to mount a compensatory tachycardia in early shock, potentially masking clinical deterioration.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Tachycardia is typically the first compensatory physiological response to developing shock.

questions.explanation: A serum lactate above 1.5 mmol/L does reflect anaerobic metabolism from inadequate tissue oxygenation and supports a clinical diagnosis of shock. Patients receiving beta-blockers may indeed fail to mount a compensatory tachycardia in early shock, since beta-blockers prevent the reflex tachycardia that would normally signal early cardiovascular decompensation, potentially masking clinical deterioration. The claim that tachycardia is typically the first compensatory physiological response to developing shock is false — a rising respiratory rate is typically the first compensatory sign, with increased minute ventilation attempting to restore oxygen delivery before tachycardia and then hypotension supervene. The claim that distributive shock typically presents with cold, clammy peripheries and a reduced cardiac output is also false — distributive shock (e.g. sepsis) is characterised by low SVR, warm or vasodilated peripheries, and a normal or high cardiac output, the opposite of the cold, low-output presentation seen in hypovolaemic or cardiogenic shock.
questions.topic: shock recognition lactate RR first beta-blockers tachycardia distributive warm high CO

questions.question: Regarding the classification and haemodynamic profiles of the four types of shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Obstructive shock may initially present with a normal or near-normal blood pressure despite critically impaired cardiac filling.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The haemodynamic definition of cardiogenic shock includes a cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m².

statements.label: C
statements.text: Neurogenic shock characteristically presents with reflex tachycardia and cold, shut-down peripheries.

statements.label: D
statements.text: All four types of shock share a final common pathway of reduced cardiac output.

questions.explanation: In early obstructive shock, compensatory mechanisms may transiently maintain a normal or near-normal blood pressure even as venous return and cardiac output are critically impaired. The haemodynamic definition of cardiogenic shock does include a cardiac index below 2.2 L/min/m², alongside systolic BP <90 mmHg and/or signs of end-organ hypoperfusion. The claim that neurogenic shock characteristically presents with reflex tachycardia and cold, shut-down peripheries is false — neurogenic shock results from loss of sympathetic outflow and instead presents with bradycardia (unopposed vagal tone) and warm, vasodilated peripheries, not the tachycardia and cold peripheries of hypovolaemic shock. The claim that all four types of shock share a final common pathway of reduced cardiac output is also false — distributive shock, the most common type, typically presents with a normal or high cardiac output and low SVR, so not all types share a low-CO final pathway.
questions.topic: shock types distributive high CO normal BP obstructive cardiogenic CI neurogenic bradycardia warm

questions.question: Regarding dynamic markers of fluid responsiveness in mechanically ventilated patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: PPV is a reliable marker of fluid responsiveness in a patient making spontaneous respiratory efforts during pressure support ventilation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A passive leg raise acts as a reversible autotransfusion and can assess fluid responsiveness without administering intravenous fluid.

statements.label: C
statements.text: PPV and stroke volume variation (SVV) are valid markers of fluid responsiveness in patients with atrial fibrillation who are on fully controlled mechanical ventilation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A pulse pressure variation (PPV) >10% predicts a positive haemodynamic response to a fluid bolus.

questions.explanation: A passive leg raise does act as a reversible autotransfusion and can assess fluid responsiveness without administering intravenous fluid, mobilising venous blood from the lower limbs to provide a fully reversible preload challenge with no net fluid given. A pulse pressure variation above 10%, measured under appropriate conditions, does predict a positive haemodynamic response to a fluid bolus. The claim that PPV is a reliable marker of fluid responsiveness in a patient making spontaneous respiratory efforts during pressure support ventilation is false — spontaneous respiratory effort during PSV generates unpredictable intrathoracic pressure changes that invalidate PPV, regardless of PEEP level or tidal volume. The claim that PPV and SVV are valid markers in patients with atrial fibrillation who are on fully controlled mechanical ventilation is also false — both sinus rhythm and fully controlled mechanical ventilation are required; atrial fibrillation generates irregular R-R intervals that invalidate the measurement even on controlled ventilation.
questions.topic: PPV SVV validity sinus rhythm controlled MV PLR reversible AF invalid spontaneous effort invalid

questions.question: Regarding randomised controlled trial evidence for fluid selection in shock resuscitation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The CHEST trial demonstrated that HES 130/0.42 significantly increased the rate of renal replacement therapy compared to 0.9% saline in ICU patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Large-volume infusion of balanced crystalloids (e.g. Hartmann's solution) carries a greater risk of hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis than 0.9% saline.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The FEAST trial showed that bolus resuscitation with albumin or 0.9% saline increased mortality compared to a no-bolus strategy in African children with severe infection.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The SAFE trial found no overall difference in 28-day mortality between 4% albumin and 0.9% saline, but a post-hoc analysis suggested higher mortality with albumin in the trauma subgroup.

questions.explanation: The CHEST trial did demonstrate that HES 130/0.42 significantly increased the rate of renal replacement therapy compared to 0.9% saline in ICU patients, with no accompanying mortality benefit. The FEAST trial showed that bolus resuscitation with either albumin or 0.9% saline increased mortality compared to a no-bolus strategy in African children with severe infection — a result contrary to expectation. The SAFE trial found no overall difference in 28-day mortality between 4% albumin and 0.9% saline, but a post-hoc analysis (SAFE-TBI) suggested higher mortality with albumin specifically among trauma patients with concomitant traumatic brain injury, not trauma patients in general. The claim that large-volume balanced crystalloids carry a greater risk of hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis than 0.9% saline is false and reversed — it is 0.9% normal saline, with its high chloride load, that causes hyperchloraemic acidosis; balanced crystalloids are preferred precisely because they avoid this complication.
questions.topic: CHEST HES renal failure SAFE albumin trauma FEAST children mortality saline acidosis balanced crystalloid

questions.question: Regarding vasopressors used in circulatory shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Dopamine is preferred over noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor in distributive shock due to its additional inotropic effects.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Vasopressin produces vasoconstriction via V1 receptors independently of the adrenergic system.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Metaraminol is an appropriate agent for sustained vasopressor support in prolonged vasodilatory septic shock.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor recommended by the Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines.

questions.explanation: Vasopressin does act via V1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle independently of adrenergic mechanisms, making it valuable as a second-line agent and in catecholamine-resistant shock; the VASST and VANISH trials established its role. Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor recommended by the Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines, with potent α1 activity and limited β-mediated side effects. The claim that dopamine is preferred over noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor due to its additional inotropic effects is false — dopamine is inferior to noradrenaline in distributive shock because it causes significantly more dysrhythmias, and its additional inotropic effect does not offset this safety disadvantage. The claim that metaraminol is an appropriate agent for sustained vasopressor support in prolonged vasodilatory septic shock is also false — metaraminol is a less potent α1 agonist suited to short-term peri-operative use, not sustained septic shock management.
questions.topic: noradrenaline first-line SSC vasopressin V1 dopamine inferior arrhythmias metaraminol short-term

questions.question: Regarding inotropic agents used in cardiogenic shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Levosimendan increases cardiac contractility by sensitising troponin C to calcium without increasing myocardial oxygen demand.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Dobutamine's β2-adrenoceptor activity can cause vasodilation, which may require co-administration of a vasopressor to maintain MAP.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Milrinone is ineffective in patients with adrenergic receptor downregulation following prolonged catecholamine infusion.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Phosphodiesterase III inhibitors (e.g. milrinone) exert their positive inotropic effect by activating β1-adrenoceptors.

questions.explanation: Levosimendan does increase cardiac contractility by sensitising troponin C to calcium, enhancing contractility without increasing intracellular calcium load or myocardial oxygen consumption. Dobutamine's β2-adrenoceptor activity can indeed cause vasodilation, which may require co-administration of a vasopressor to maintain MAP in hypotensive patients — a fall in MAP is an expected side effect here. The claim that milrinone is ineffective in patients with adrenergic receptor downregulation following prolonged catecholamine infusion is false — because milrinone completely bypasses adrenoceptors, it retains efficacy even when β1 downregulation has rendered catecholamines ineffective; this is in fact its primary clinical advantage in that setting. The claim that phosphodiesterase III inhibitors such as milrinone exert their inotropic effect by activating β1-adrenoceptors is also false — milrinone and other PDE III inhibitors work by inhibiting cAMP breakdown, acting independently of adrenoceptors; they do not activate β1 receptors.
questions.topic: dobutamine β2 vasodilation milrinone PDE3 adrenoceptor independent levosimendan calcium sensitiser downregulation

questions.question: Regarding methods of cardiac output measurement in the critically ill:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The pulmonary artery catheter, using thermodilution or dye dilution, is considered the gold standard for cardiac output measurement.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Transthoracic echocardiography can rapidly exclude pericardial tamponade as a cause of obstructive shock at the bedside.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The LiDCOrapid monitor derives cardiac output from arterial waveform morphology and does not require calibration against an external reference.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Oesophageal Doppler directly measures whole-body cardiac output without requiring any correction for anatomical distribution of flow.

questions.explanation: The pulmonary artery catheter, using thermodilution or dye dilution, remains the reference standard against which all other cardiac output monitors are validated, though routine use has declined due to insertion risks and a lack of demonstrated mortality benefit in RCTs. Transthoracic echocardiography can indeed rapidly exclude pericardial tamponade as a cause of obstructive shock at the bedside, identifying pericardial effusion with cardiac chamber collapse within minutes. The LiDCOrapid monitor does derive cardiac output from arterial waveform morphology without requiring calibration against an external reference, unlike PiCCO and LiDCO, which require thermodilution or lithium dilution calibration. The claim that oesophageal Doppler directly measures whole-body cardiac output without requiring any correction for anatomical distribution of flow is false — oesophageal Doppler measures flow only in the descending thoracic aorta, which carries approximately 70% of cardiac output; the remaining 30% is distributed to the head, upper limbs, and coronary circulation proximal to the probe, so a correction factor is always required.
questions.topic: PAC gold standard oesophageal Doppler 70% correction LiDCOrapid uncalibrated TTE tamponade

questions.question: Regarding goal-directed therapy (GDT) and haemodynamic targets in shock resuscitation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Current evidence supports a standardised, protocol-driven approach to haemodynamic targets over an individualised physiological assessment.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The ARISE, ProCESS, and ProMISe trials confirmed a mortality benefit from routine protocolised EGDT in multicentre practice.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Both excessive and insufficient fluid administration are independently associated with increased morbidity in critically ill patients.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Rivers et al. (2001) reported a significant mortality benefit from early goal-directed therapy targeting ScvO₂, CVP, and MAP in a single-centre RCT of patients with early septic shock.

questions.explanation: Rivers et al. (2001) did report a significant mortality benefit from early goal-directed therapy targeting ScvO2, CVP, and MAP, in a landmark single-centre RCT of patients with early septic shock. Both excessive and insufficient fluid administration are independently associated with increased morbidity in critically ill patients — this bidirectional harm (excess causing oedema and organ dysfunction, deficit causing ischaemia) underscores the need for dynamic ongoing assessment. The claim that the ARISE, ProCESS, and ProMISe trials confirmed a mortality benefit from routine protocolised EGDT in multicentre practice is false — these three large international multicentre RCTs all showed no mortality benefit from routine EGDT versus usual care, refuting protocol-driven resuscitation in the general ICU sepsis population. The claim that current evidence supports a standardised, protocol-driven approach over an individualised physiological assessment is also false — current consensus, informed by these RCTs, strongly favours an individualised approach rather than a fixed protocol.
questions.topic: Rivers EGDT ARISE ProCESS ProMISe no benefit individualised approach fluid harm bidirectional

questions.question: This pie chart shows the relative frequency of circulatory shock aetiologies among patients presenting with arterial hypotension (Vincent & De Backer, NEJM 2013). Which statement is correct?
options: A: Non-septic distributive shock is the least common aetiology of hypotension shown here
B: Obstructive shock is more common than non-septic distributive shock as a cause of arterial hypotension
C: Cardiogenic and hypovolemic shock are roughly equal in frequency, together about half as common as septic shock
D: Hypovolemic shock is more common than cardiogenic shock as a cause of arterial hypotension
E: Distributive (septic) shock alone accounts for less than half of all cases shown
questions.explanation: The figure shows septic (distributive) shock as the most common cause (62%), with cardiogenic and hypovolemic shock tied at 16% each — together (32%) roughly half of the septic proportion, matching the correct answer here. Non-septic distributive shock (4%) is more common than obstructive shock (2%), so the claim that non-septic distributive shock is the least common cause shown is false — obstructive shock, not non-septic distributive, is the least common. The claim that obstructive shock is more common than non-septic distributive shock is also false, for the same reason in reverse: obstructive (2%) is less common than non-septic distributive (4%). Hypovolaemic and cardiogenic shock are tied at 16% each, so neither is more common than the other. Septic shock alone (62%) is well over half of all cases shown, so the claim that it accounts for less than half is false.
questions.topic: shock aetiology relative frequency epidemiology pie chart distributive cardiogenic hypovolemic obstructive
